content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660              322-5901           322-6441            322-6444

March 2, 1984


James Gross
Nielsen, Hodgson, Parrinello
& Mueller
1225 Eighth Street, Suite 550
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Advice Letter No. A-84-040

Dear Jim,

This letter is to confirm my oral advice to you on
February 29, 1984, regarding Government Code Section
87103(c).1/

FACTS

A public official made a loan of $250 or more to an
individual who promised, but failed, to repay the loan.
Thirteen months after the original promise, the official is
confronted with a decision which will foreseeably have a
material financial effect on the borrower.

QUESTION PRESENTED

Is the official required to disqualify himself on the
decision?

DISCUSSION

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, or
participating in the making of, a governmental decision in which
he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. An
official has a "financial interest" in a decision within the

_________________________

1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code.

James Gross
March 2, 1984
Page 2


meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect on:

* * *

(c) Any source of income . . . aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided
to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made.

(Section 87103(c).)

In the situation you presented, the official must disqualify
nimself from a decision which will foreseeably have a material
effect on the borrower because $250 or more of the debt was owed
during the 12 months prior to the decision. Although the
original promise to repay the loan occurred 13 months before the
decision, the promise to repay the loan continues as long as a
sufficient amount of the loan remains outstanding.2/

Disqualification is also required if, during the 12 months
prior to the decision, the official received payments on the
loan amounting to $250 or more.

If I can be of any additional help to you, please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis
Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:plh

_____________________

2/ However, if the official writes the obligation off as
a bad debt for tax purposes, or if he forgives the debt, the
promise to pay will end and, after 12 months pass, the borrower
will no longer be a source of income.

decision?
sections.conclusion: In the situation you presented, the official must disqualify himself from a decision which will foreseeably have a material affect on the borrower because $250 or more of the debt was owed during the 12 months prior to the decision. Although the original promise to repay the loan occurred 13 months before the decision, the promise to repay the loan continues as long as a sufficient amount of the loan remains outstanding. Disqualification is also required if, during the 12 months prior to the decision, the official received payments on the loan amounting to $250 or more.

Thirteen months after the original promise, the official is 
confronted with a decision which will foreseeably have a 
material financial effect on the borrower.

participating in the making of, a governmental decision in which 
he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. 
An 
official has a "financial interest" in a decision within the

James Gross

meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a public official must disqualify themselves from a decision that will materially affect a borrower when a $250+ loan was made 13 months prior and remains outstanding.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Yes, the official must disqualify themselves because the outstanding debt of $250+ constitutes a financial interest under Government Code Section 87103(c), as the obligation to repay continues during the relevant 12-month period prior to the decision.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Clear advice letter format with standard Q/C structure. Document contains footnote clarifying that write-off or forgiveness of debt would terminate the financial interest after 12 months pass. Minor OCR artifact in phone number ('322-6-4.-41') does not affect substantive content extraction.

QUESTION: Is the official required to disqualify himself on the 
decision?

CONCLUSION: In the situation you presented, the official must disqualify himself from a decision which will foreseeably have a material affect on the borrower because $250 or more of the debt was owed during the 12 months prior to the decision. Although the original promise to repay the loan occurred 13 months before the decision, the promise to repay the loan continues as long as a sufficient amount of the loan remains outstanding. Disqualification is also required if, during the 12 months prior to the decision, the official received payments on the loan amounting to $250 or more.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter confirming oral guidance that a public official must disqualify themselves from decisions affecting a borrower when an outstanding loan of $250+ was promised within 12 months prior to the decision, as this constitutes a financial interest under Government Code Section 87103(c).
